blocks.text: Procurement, Rewritten as Code

Before CPOs automate sourcing with AI “skills,” they must first build a governance layer that exposes every assumption, confidence score, and version history—turning automation into a transparent decision-aid rather than a source of persuasive error.

For decades, procurement has been described in the language of process. Stage gates. Templates. Approval flows. Yet anyone who has worked inside it knows that the real work happens elsewhere: in judgement calls, in incomplete information, in conversations that never make it into the file.

Artificial intelligence has begun to enter this space. So far, its role has been modest—drafting, summarising, assisting. But a different idea is now emerging. Not AI as assistant, but AI as **operator**.
The concept is called a “skill.”
From prompts to capabilities

Most interactions with AI begin with a prompt: an instruction, a question, a request. Prompts are flexible, but fragile. They depend on phrasing, context, and user skill. The same question can yield wildly different answers.
A skill takes a different approach. It defines, in advance: * what it is responsible for * how it should reason * what it must produce * and under which assumptions it operates

In that sense, a skill behaves less like a conversation and more like a function. But it would be a mistake to push the analogy too far. A skill is not a specialist. It does not improvise, intuit, or draw on experience beyond what has been encoded. It is better understood as a **codified operator under defined assumptions**—powerful within its boundaries, brittle outside them.
> **The technology works only if the human remains actively engaged.** > Governance is not an after-thought; it is the prerequisite.
A procurement function, decomposed

Take a sourcing exercise. It begins with an unclear need. It moves through market analysis, supplier identification, qualification, negotiation, and ultimately a decision. In the skills model, each of these steps becomes a discrete capability:

* one structures the intake and clarifies the brief * another assesses complexity and determines the appropriate level of effort * others scan the market, build supplier longlists, and qualify candidates * further skills construct RFX packages, evaluate bids, and prepare negotiations * a final skill supports the award decision
Overseeing all of this is a conductor—an orchestration layer that determines which capabilities to activate, and when to step back.
The ambition is not to replace procurement professionals, but to **make parts of procurement executable**.
The illusion of authority
What distinguishes this approach is discipline. Each skill carries: * a playbook for reasoning * a schema defining its output * embedded domain references * and validation logic

The result is structured output—artifacts rather than suggestions. That shift is meaningful. Artifacts can be reviewed, reused, audited. But it introduces a new risk. A well-structured output carries an aura of authority. It looks complete. It reads as final. And that makes errors harder to detect.
A human analyst may say, *“I’m not sure.”* A system rarely does—unless explicitly designed to. Without mechanisms to surface: * assumptions * confidence levels * and blind spots
structured output becomes something more dangerous than guesswork: **it becomes persuasive error.**
Governance as lightweight playbook
To counter that risk, CPOs need a minimal but non-negotiable governance layer. The template is simple:

| Element | Example | Purpose | |---------|---------|---------| | **Assumption log** | “Supplier X is Tier 1 [data: 2023 audit]” | Exposes hidden premises | | **Confidence score** | “78 %” | Flags low-reliability outputs | | **Version history** | “v2.3 – updated 2024-05-12” | Enables rollback and audit | | **Red-flag threshold** | “Escalate if confidence <80 %” | Forces human review |
These four fields fit in a single YAML block or a Git commit message. Lightweight, but sufficient to turn a black box into an inspectable decision-aid.
Governance, adaptability—and memory
There is a familiar promise here. Enterprise systems have long claimed to offer: * standardisation without rigidity * best practices that evolve over time
History suggests otherwise. Processes calcify. “Best practice” ages quickly. Change depends on resources that procurement teams do not control.

Skills risk repeating this pattern—unless they are treated differently. Their advantage is not that they impose structure. It is that they are **lightweight, versionable, and explicit**. A skill can be rewritten, tested, and improved in a way a process document rarely is.
But that depends on organisation, not architecture. Without active ownership, even the most elegant SKILL.md becomes just another file—precise, well-written, and quietly outdated.
Composability in an unstructured world

The idea of a “composable procurement function” borrows from software engineering. But procurement is not software. Its inputs are not clean: * the brief is negotiated * the market is interpreted * the data is partial
The skills model does not eliminate this ambiguity. At best, it manages it. The intake step, for example, does not assume clarity. It creates it—through guided questioning, iteration, and refinement.

In that sense, composability is not about perfect inputs. It is about **progressively structuring ambiguity**. Even so, the system is most reliable where procurement is already structured—which is why sourcing is the starting point. The more strategic, relational, and context-dependent areas remain harder to encode.
Where judgement goes
Proponents of this model argue that human effort moves “upstream”: away from execution, towards supervision and orchestration. This is plausible. It is also fragile.

Supervision requires judgement. Judgement requires experience. If professionals become accustomed to accepting structured outputs without challenge, the underlying competence risks erosion. The system, in that scenario, does not augment expertise. It replaces it—quietly, and incompletely.
The alternative is more demanding. It requires professionals to: * interrogate outputs * challenge assumptions * and treat the system as a counterpart, not an authority
In other words, the system does not remove responsibility. It raises the standard for exercising it.
What is gained—and what is exposed

The most compelling aspect of the skills approach is not automation. It is exposure. By forcing: * reasoning into explicit steps * outputs into defined structures * and assumptions into something that can be inspected
it makes procurement more transparent than it has traditionally been. Tacit knowledge becomes visible. Inconsistencies become detectable. Improvements become possible.
But visibility cuts both ways. It exposes not only strengths, but gaps—including those the system cannot fill. Skills make reasoning visible, but they do not make responsibility clear.
A beginning, not a conclusion

The current implementations focus on sourcing for a reason. It is the most structured part of procurement, with the clearest inputs and outputs. That makes it the most tractable. It also makes it an incomplete test.
The harder domains—supplier relationships, risk, ethics, geopolitics—remain resistant to codification. Whether skills can extend meaningfully into those areas is an open question.
The more useful question
It is tempting to frame this shift as inevitable. It is not. The more useful question is narrower, and more demanding:
> Which parts of procurement benefit from becoming programmable—and which parts lose something essential in the process?
Skills offer one answer. They make certain capabilities: * repeatable * testable * and improvable
But they also introduce new risks: * false confidence * hidden blind spots * and diffused accountability
Closing thought

The history of business is, in part, the history of abstraction—from manual work to processes, from processes to systems. Skills suggest a further step. Not the replacement of procurement expertise, but its **translation into something that can be executed, inspected, and improved**.
The question is not whether procurement will become more programmable. The question is whether the people inside it will remain legible to themselves when it does.
